user_request: Draft a suit for damages for defamation against a Defendant who published false and malicious allegations on Facebook damaging the Plaintiff's business reputation. Reproduce the defamatory statements in the body of the plaint. Plead malice, publication to third parties, loss of goodwill, and mental agony. Claim Rs.10,00,000/- as damages. Include prayer for permanent injunction restraining further publication on Facebook or any other social media platform. Draft suitable for filing before the District Court. Plead limitation correctly for defamation suits. Include cause of action paragraph with accrual date, continuing nature of cause of action, valuation, court fee, and proper verification clause.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks damages for defamation against a Defendant who published false and malicious allegations on Facebook, damaging the Plaintiff's business reputation. The suit includes claims for loss of goodwill, mental agony, and a permanent injunction against further publication.

assumptions: Jurisdiction lies with the District Court based on Plaintiff's residence or place of publication
Cause of action is continuing due to online publication

An Act to consolidate and amend the law for the limitation of suits and other proceedings and for

ACT NO. 36 OF 1963 
[5th October, 1963.] 
An Act to consolidate and amend the law for the limitation of suits and other proceedings and for 
purposes connected therewith. 
BE it enacted by Parliament in the Fourteenth Year of the Republic of India as follows:—

the ground that the property was not correctly valued on the basis of the fact that while giving description of the 
property in the sale deed, the market value of the property was given much higher almost double the sale price. 1 
Where the valuation of suit property by the plaintiff is arbitrary and unreasonable, the court can interfere with it 
under O 7, r 11.2
In a case where defendant moved an application under O 7, r 11 of the Code for rejection of the plaint for not 
affixing proper court-fees at the state when the case was fixed for evidence, was as held by the Punjab and 
Haryana High Court that dismissed of the application on the ground that issue had been framed regarding court-fee 
and the same would be decided alongwith other issued on the basis of evidence led by parties, was not improper 
exercise of jurisdiction. It was observed that mere error or wrong exercise of jurisdiction cannot entitle the defendant 
to invoke Art. 227 of the Constitution.3
The Court has power at any stage of the proceedings to direct the plaintiff to affix the court-fees according to the 
damages assessed by it in a suit for rendition of accounts. In cases of undervaluation or improper fraction of the 
court-fee, the

[Document 2 | CPC | CPC:books/491014025-MULLA-on-CPC-pdf.pdf:P1791:C7139 | commentary | 491014025-MULLA-on-CPC-pdf | p.1791 | score=0.668]:
rt must accept the plaintiffs valuation. 85 If the correct valuation would render the court incompetent to 
entertain the suit, cl (b) does not apply. In such a case, the plaint must be returned under r 10 to be presented to 
the proper court and the deficient court-fee will be paid in the court having jurisdiction to hear the suit. 86 In 
considering the question of correct valuation, the court is not confined to what appears on the plaint. It can, rely on 
admissions made in interlocutory proceedings.87 When a suit is not correctly valued, the appropriate order to pass is 
to call upon the plaintiff to give the correct valuation and no order should be passed at that stage for payment of the 
deficient court-fee.88 A composite order requiring the party to give the correct valuation and to pay deficient court-
fee is not warranted.89 Where the court holds that the relief has been undervalued and orders it to be corrected, it is 
open to the plaintiff to amend the valuation at any time before an order rejecting the plaint is made so as to limit the 
claim to the court-fee paid.90 In suit for accounts, in coming to the conclusion that the relief is undervalued the court 
will have to take into account that in a suit for account the plaintiff is not obliged to state exact amount which would 
result after the taking of accounts. If he cannot estimate the exact amount he can put a

t 
of deficit court-fees.19
An order rejecting a plaint under this rule should be made only after the plaintiff is given an opportunity to correct 
the valuation and pay the deficient court-fee and he fails to do so.20
Thus, in a suit for permanent injunction and restraining the defendant from putting up construction in the house 
agreed upon to be sold, the plaintiff amended the plaint to include additional relief of specific performance of sale 
agreement, but the requisite court-fee for additional relief was not paid. The order rejecting the plaint partly in 
respect of the additional relief was not challenged. Subsequent payment of court-fee is not valid payment. 21 In 
another case of injunction for restraining the electricity board from disconnecting the electric supply, the suit was 
valued at Rs 600 but fixed court-fee of Rs 60 was paid. It was held that order directing the plaintiff to pay ad 
valorem court-fee was legal. The High Courts observed that mere astuteness in drafting the plaint would not glaze 
the jurisdiction of court for looking at the substance of the relief claimed for.22
The procedure prescribed by the present rule is not applicable to a case in which the Appellate Court acts under s 
12 of the Court Fees Act 1870. 23 There is no provision for recovering court-fees determined as payable under this 
rule, as the plaint is treated, when court-fee is

[Document 4 | CPC | CPC:books/491014025-MULLA-on-CPC-pdf.pdf:P47:C180 | commentary | 491014025-MULLA-on-CPC-pdf | p.47 | score=0.545]:
limits of the courts jurisdiction, the court may pass a decree for the sum in 
excess of the pecuniary limits of its jurisdiction. 10 The court has no jurisdiction to grant reliefs not claimed in the 
plaint if by reason of their inclusion, the suit would be beyond the pecuniary jurisdiction of the court. 11 Where 
different suits which are severally within the jurisdiction of the court are consolidated for hearing, the court does 
not lose its jurisdiction to hear it by reason of the value of all the suits exceeding its jurisdiction if taken 
together.12
The Bombay High Court has held that in a case of damages, the amount of damage which the Court can award 
is limited to the pecuniary jurisdiction of the Court.13
ILLUSTRATION
A sues B in the court of second class subordinate judge for an account and values his suit for a court fee at Rs 
130. Under s 8 of the Suits Valuation Act, 1887, the value of the subject matter of the suit is Rs 130. The parties 
arrive at a compromise whereby the first defendant is to pay Rs 6,000 and the second defendant Rs 5,000 to 
the plaintiff. The court may pass a decree in terms of the compromise, although it has jurisdiction limited to suits 
of which the value of the subject matter does not exceed Rs 5,000.14
The forum of appeal also is determined by the value of the suit and not by the amount decreed.15

ngs to direct the plaintiff to affix the court-fees according to the 
damages assessed by it in a suit for rendition of accounts. In cases of undervaluation or improper fraction of the 
court-fee, the Court has ample power to direct a party to affix a proper court-fee. Therefore, in a suit for rendition of 
accounts, where the plaintiff-respondent was not aware of the exact profit earned by the defendant, it was held that 
the plaint cannot be rejected at the threshold for want of proper court-fees.4
9. Clause (c): Where a plaint is written upon paper insufficiently stamped. The following points are to be noted 
in connection with this clause:
(i) Where a plaint is written upon paper insufficiently stamped, the court is bound to give the plaintiff time to make 
good the deficiency. This follows from the terms of cl (c) itself. 5 Reasonable time must be allowed after the court 
has decided that the court-fee paid is insufficient, 6 and within the time allowed, if the plaintiff cannot pay, he may 
apply to continue the suit as a pauper.7
(ii) If the plaintiff fails to supply the requisite stamp-paper within the period fixed by the court, the plaint may be 
rejected under this rule, even after it has been numbered and registered as a suit. The reason is that the power to 
reject a plaint under this rule is not exhausted when the plaint has been admitted and registered.8

proper court having pecuniary jurisdiction, even if a higher court-fee is paid.60

draft_artifacts.title: Plaint for damages for defamation
draft_artifacts.text:
In the District Court of {{PLACE_NAME}} at {{CITY_NAME}}
Civil Suit No. ____________ of 20__
Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, resident of {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as 'Plaintiff').
Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, resident of {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as 'Defendant').

JURISDICTION AND VENUE
This Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to entertain and try this suit in respect of defamation arising within its territorial jurisdiction and is properly vested with the subject matter hereunder. The Plaintiff states facts within the jurisdiction of this Court and the cause of action arises within the said jurisdiction.

FACTS AND DEFAMATORY ACTS
1. The Plaintiff seeks damages for defamation against the Defendant who published false and malicious statements on the social media platform Facebook, thereby harming the Plaintiff's business reputation.
2. The exact defamatory statements published by the Defendant on Facebook are reproduced below for the purposes of this plaint: '{{PLACEHOLDER_DEFAMATORY_STATEMENTS}}' (the text is to be read as an authentic reproduction of the offending statements). The publication date is {{PUB_DATE}}.
3. The publication was made by the Defendant with malice and was communicated to third parties who could access and read the same on Facebook. The Plaintiff contends that the statements were intended to belittle the Plaintiff in the eyes of the public and to cause reputational harm to the Plaintiff's business activities.
4. As a direct consequence of the said publication, the Plaintiff has suffered loss of goodwill, mental agony, and distress.
5. The Plaintiff seeks a permanent injunction restraining the Defendant from making or causing further publication of any defamatory material on Facebook or any other social media platform, including but not limited to Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube, or through any other electronic or print medium.

CAUSE OF ACTION (ACCRUAL, CONTINUING NATURE, AND LIMITATION)
6. The cause of action for defamation arose on the date of the first publication on Facebook, namely {{ACCRUAL_DATE}}. The Plaintiff states that the right to sue accrued on that date.
7. The cause of action is of a continuing nature due to ongoing and repeated publications by the Defendant on Facebook and other social media platforms since the date of first publication. The Plaintiff pleads continuing publication as a continuing cause of action and seeks relief for damages arising therefrom.
8. Limitation: The Plaintiff asserts that the suit is within the applicable limitation period for civil defamation suits; the precise limitation period and accrual rule shall be verified and stated clearly at the time of filing, with due regard to the governing provisions applicable in this jurisdiction. {{LIMITATION_PERIOD}} (to be verified before filing).

VALUE, DAMAGES, AND COURT FEES
9. The Plaintiff claims damages in the sum of Rs. 10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh Only) for defamation, loss of goodwill, mental agony, and injury to business reputation.
10. The value of the subject matter for the purpose of court fees and jurisdiction is Rs. 10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh Only).
11. Court fee payable on the suit value: to be calculated per COURT_FEE_CONTEXT. The applicable rate and computation method shall be stated as {{COURT_FEE_RATE}} and the amount shall be {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} (to be verified at the time of filing).

PRAYER AND RELIEF SOUGHT
In the premises, the Plaintiff prays for:
(a) A declaration that the Defendant has defamed the Plaintiff by publishing false and malicious statements on Facebook and other social media;
(b) A decree for damages in the sum of Rs. 10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh Only) along with such further interest and costs as the Court deems fit;
(c) A permanent injunction restraining the Defendant from making or causing further publication of any defamatory material on Facebook or any other social media platform, or through any other medium;
(d) Costs of the suit; and
(e) Any other relief that this Hon'ble Court may deem fit in the facts and circumstances of the case.

VERIFICATION
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff in the above matter, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief and that I have not suppressed any material facts.

Place: {{PLACE_NAME}}
Date: {{DATE}}

affirmed by the Plaintiff

Signed: ______________________
Name: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}
Address: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}

LIST OF DOCUMENTS / ANNEXURES

draft_artifacts.title: Plaint for damages for defamation
draft_artifacts.text:
In the District Court of {{PLACE_NAME}} at {{CITY_NAME}}
Civil Suit No. ____________ of 20__
Plaintiff: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, resident of {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as 'Plaintiff').
Defendant: {{DEFENDANT_NAME}}, resident of {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}} (hereinafter referred to as 'Defendant').

JURISDICTION AND VENUE
This Hon'ble Court has jurisdiction to entertain and try this suit in respect of defamation arising within its territorial jurisdiction and is properly vested with the subject matter hereunder. The Plaintiff states facts within the jurisdiction of this Court and the cause of action arises within the said jurisdiction.

FACTS AND DEFAMATORY ACTS
1. The Plaintiff seeks damages for defamation against the Defendant who published false and malicious statements on the social media platform Facebook, thereby harming the Plaintiff's business reputation.
2. The exact defamatory statements published by the Defendant on Facebook are reproduced in Annexure A (the text is to be read as an authentic reproduction). The date of first publication is to be read as per Annexure A.
3. The publication was made by the Defendant with malice and was communicated to third parties who could access and read the same on Facebook. The Plaintiff contends that the statements were intended to belittle the Plaintiff in the eyes of the public and to cause reputational harm to the Plaintiff's business activities.
4. As a direct consequence of the said publication, the Plaintiff has suffered loss of goodwill, mental agony, and distress.
5. The Plaintiff seeks a permanent injunction restraining the Defendant from making or causing further publication of any defamatory material on Facebook or any other social media platform, including but not limited to Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube, or through any other electronic or print medium.

CAUSE OF ACTION (ACCRUAL, CONTINUING NATURE, AND LIMITATION)
6. The cause of action for defamation arose on the date of first publication on Facebook, i.e., the date of first publication as per Annexure A. The Plaintiff states that the right to sue accrued on that date.
7. The cause of action is of a continuing nature due to ongoing publications by the Defendant on Facebook and other social media platforms since the date of first publication. The Plaintiff pleads continuing publication as a continuing cause of action and seeks relief for damages arising therefrom.
8. Limitation: The suit is within the applicable limitation period for civil defamation suits; the precise limitation period and accrual rule shall be verified and stated clearly at the time of filing, with due regard to the governing provisions applicable in this jurisdiction. {{LIMITATION_PERIOD}} (to be verified before filing).

VALUE, DAMAGES, AND COURT FEES
9. The Plaintiff claims damages in the sum of Rs. 10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh Only) for defamation, loss of goodwill, mental agony, and injury to business reputation.
10. The value of the subject matter for the purpose of court fees and jurisdiction is Rs. 10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh Only).
11. Court fee payable on the suit value: to be calculated per the applicable Court Fee Schedule; the specific rate and amount shall be provided at the time of filing: {{COURT_FEE_RATE}} and {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}} (to be verified at the time of filing).

PRAYER AND RELIEF SOUGHT
In the premises, the Plaintiff prays for:
(a) A declaration that the Defendant has defamed the Plaintiff by publishing false and malicious statements on Facebook and other social media;
(b) A decree for damages in the sum of Rs. 10,00,000 (Rupees Ten Lakh Only) along with such further interest and costs as the Court deems fit;
(c) A permanent injunction restraining the Defendant from making or causing further publication of any defamatory material on Facebook or any other social media platform, or through any other medium;
(d) Costs of the suit; and
(e) Any other relief that this Hon'ble Court may deem fit in the facts and circumstances of the case.

VERIFICATION
I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff in the above matter, do hereby verify that the contents of this plaint are true to my knowledge and belief and that I have not suppressed any material facts.

Place: {{PLACE_NAME}}
Date: {{DATE}}

affirmed by the Plaintiff

Signed: ______________________
Name: {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}
Address: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}}

LIST OF DOCUMENTS / ANNEXURES
Annexure A: Copy/screenshot of the defamatory post published on Facebook (verbatim text to be read as reproduced).
Annexure B: Any other corroborative material relied upon, if any.
